content.text: modifier prevents a class field from being assigned after the object is constructed. It can still be set during declaration (field initialiser) or inside the constructor. Once the constructor returns, any attempt to re-assign the field is a compile-time error — TypeScript catches it before the code ever runs.

attrs.alt: readonly field assignment: allowed in constructor and initialiser, blocked everywhere else

readonly makes the reference immutable, not the object. For deeply immutable data, use Readonly<T> or as const.

If a base class field is
, a subclass can widen it to non-readonly by re-declaring it — but this defeats the parent's guarantee. Avoid this pattern unless you have a very specific reason.
When building test fixtures or spy objects for classes with
fields, use a type cast through
to populate the fields, then cast to the real type. This avoids creating full constructor hierarchies in tests.
Angular Router and Typed readonly Routes
in Angular is creating a typed route constants object. This gives auto-completion on route strings and a single source of truth.
fields can only be assigned in their declaration or the constructor — any later assignment is a compile error.

content: readonly and const are analogous but apply to class fields vs variable bindings respectively.
Neither readonly nor const prevents mutation of the referenced object — only re-assignment of the variable/field.

content.text: marks all properties of a type readonly without changing the underlying class.

content: In Angular: use readonly for injected services in the constructor shorthand, expose Signals as .asReadonly(), and mark @Input() objects Readonly<T> to prevent child-component mutations.
